summary: The test suite provides strong behavioral coverage of the worktree-cleanup interactive selection feature. It tests the primary flow (no args → interactive selection → cleanup), multi-select, backward compatibility with explicit slug, empty selection, no worktrees available, git failure, and structural extension shape. The tests are realistic, executable with node:test (matching repo conventions), and make meaningful behavioral assertions against a well-constructed mock context.

findings.title: ctx.ui.custom API shape is heavily assumed
findings.details: The entire test suite assumes ctx.ui.custom takes a renderer callback with (tui, theme, keybindings, done) signature, and that done() receives an array of slug strings. The plan references a 'pi inline thingy' pattern from /ss command but does not specify the exact API. If the actual implementation uses a different pattern (e.g., ctx.ui.select, or done receives paths instead of slugs), all interactive tests will need rewriting.
findings.fix: This is acknowledged in ambiguousRequirements and the tests are structured to surface diagnostic failures. No immediate fix needed, but consider adding a comment noting which tests would need updating if the API shape differs.
findings.requirement: Critical behavior remains ambiguous and needs clarification before implementation.
evidence: The structural smoke test and getCleanupCommand helper provide good diagnostics if assumptions are wrong.

findings.title: Double-done race in first interactive test
findings.details: In the first interactive test ('worktree-cleanup without args lists available worktrees...'), the renderer callback immediately calls done via the passed function, AND the setTimeout also calls done. Unlike the later tests, this first test does NOT have a `resolved` guard flag, so done/resolve could be called twice. While Promise.resolve ignoring subsequent calls is safe in practice, it's inconsistent with the Fix 1 pattern applied to all other tests.
findings.fix: Add the `resolved` guard flag pattern to the first interactive test's ui.custom mock, matching the pattern used in all subsequent tests.
evidence: First test: `const done = (result: T) => resolve(result);` with no guard
Later tests: `let resolved = false; const done = (result: T) => { if (!resolved) { resolved = true; resolve(result); } };`

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/currently-it-is-not-possible-for-me-to-work-easily-on-the-sa.plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

strengths: Comprehensive behavioral coverage: primary flow, multi-select, backward compat, empty selection, no worktrees, git failure
Good diagnostic smoke test that logs actual export keys on failure
Uses node:test matching repo conventions rather than blindly following framework metadata
Meaningful negative assertions (e.g., verifying unselected worktrees are NOT removed, UI is NOT shown when slug provided)
Edge cases well covered: empty selection notifies user, only-main-worktree notifies user, git failure handled gracefully
getCleanupCommand helper provides clear diagnostic message if export shape differs
